lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS231N | Spring 2025 | Lecture 4: Neural Networks and Backpropagation

unit.name: Computational graphs and basic backpropagation
unit.description: Introduces computational graphs as the representation underlying automatic gradient computation and walks through the simplest backprop example.
unit.summary: After noting that deriving full gradients on paper is tedious and can become infeasible when the loss is modified or the graph is complex, the lecture introduces computational graphs as a better representation for composing scores, losses, regularizers, and parameters into one graph from inputs to scalar loss [ts=2400s] [ts=2462s] [ts=2488s]. Backpropagation is then presented as the mechanism for traversing that graph backward to compute all needed derivatives [ts=2591s] [ts=2624s]. The first worked example uses f(x, y, z) = (x + y)z, decomposed into an add node and a multiply node, and shows how backprop starts from df/df = 1 at the output, uses direct local derivatives where possible, and applies the chain rule when variables are not directly connected [ts=2643s] [ts=2820s] [ts=2894s]. This motivates the vocabulary of upstream, local, and downstream gradients, with each node multiplying upstream gradient by its local gradient and passing the result backward [ts=2953s] [ts=3118s].

key_points.text: Computational graphs represent the operations from inputs and parameters all the way to the final scalar loss.
Backpropagation is the solution that makes gradient computation tractable on complex computational graphs.
The simple example f(x, y, z) = (x + y)z is decomposed into an addition node followed by a multiplication node.
Backprop starts at the output with the derivative of the quantity with respect to itself equal to 1.
Upstream gradient comes from later nodes, local gradient comes from the current node’s operation, and their product yields downstream gradients.
A downstream gradient for one node becomes the upstream gradient for earlier nodes in the graph.

kp_catalog.name: Computational graphs and automatic differentiation
kp_catalog.description: General reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation. A neural network can be understood as a directed acyclic graph of operations whose forward pass computes values and whose backward pass computes gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because computational graphs and automatic differentiation formalizes how general reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Backpropagation as recursive chain rule
kp_catalog.description: The backward traversal of a computational graph that repeatedly applies the chain rule to obtain gradients for all relevant variables and parameters.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because backpropagation as recursive chain rule formalizes how the backward traversal of a computational graph that repeatedly applies the chain rule to obtain gradients for all relevant variables and parameters, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Local, upstream, and downstream gradients
kp_catalog.description: The node-level view of backprop where each node multiplies its local derivative by an incoming upstream gradient to produce gradients for earlier nodes.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because local, upstream, and downstream gradients clarifies the node-level view of backprop where each node multiplies its local derivative by an incoming upstream gradient to produce gradients for earlier nodes, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
